This post is more implementation-focused, so I won't spend too much time on theory. Let's start building the HTTP API step by step.

"What about real-world dev? Is this how backend API development really looks?"

Of course not — I just wanted to show how HTTP APIs work, so I implemented it in pure Node.js. In actual backend API development, we usually use frameworks like Express, Koa, Fastify, etc. These frameworks handle a lot of stuff for us — routing, error handling, data validation, etc. — so we can focus on business logic instead of dealing with these things.
We saw earlier that we always hit
in Postman. But in real-world dev, we don't usually have just one route. We have many —
, etc. These are different routes. So now we'll implement routing.
The example will use a TodoList. We'll have these routes:
I won't go through all of them, since the concepts are similar. I'll only cover
— try implementing the others yourself as homework.
This example stores data in memory, so when you close the server the data disappears. We're just demonstrating routing — no database needed.

What's memory? Imagine writing things on paper. When you turn off the computer, you're throwing the paper away — your data disappears. If you keep the paper in a drawer, your data is preserved. That's a simple analogy for memory vs database.
